rationales.hedonism: She keeps postponing the Alibaug trip and says the saved dates feel "like some kind of joke," showing pleasure keeps getting deferred.
rationales.security: She spends the evening "comparing fixed deposits" and agrees they should lock in a rate before it drops, prioritizing financial stability.
rationales.benevolence: The trip is repeatedly sacrificed for "school fees" and Anil's mother needing "new glasses," putting close family needs first.

rationales.self_direction: She rejects her sister's judgment, saying "I do not need her opinion on how we spend our time or money," asserting her own choices.
rationales.conformity: Her response pushes back against family pressure about what a "proper holiday" should look like instead of accommodating that expectation.

rationales.security: She treats the dental visit as a relief because "the last round cost us eleven thousand rupees" and immediately arranges tuition, focusing on practical stability.
rationales.benevolence: Her day centers on caring for the children, from checkups to homework to arranging extra help for Aarav.

rationales.self_direction: She wants choices that "do not require a committee meeting," pushing for more personal control over small decisions.
rationales.hedonism: She imagines "booking that resort in Goa," buying "the good cheese," and taking an auto with the kids, explicitly wanting more comfort and enjoyment.
rationales.security: She pushes back on yet another protection-focused plan, asking "At what point do we stop saving for some imagined disaster and use the money to actually live a little?"

rationales.hedonism: She says that even when the party goes right, "there is nothing in it for me," showing her own enjoyment is being crowded out.
rationales.benevolence: She pays almost double for the castle cake because she "could not bear the thought of my daughter's face when there is no castle cake," prioritizing her child over cost and stress.

rationales.hedonism: The hidden wine on the balcony and "forty-five minutes of nothing" are described as "the best part of my week," clearly valuing private pleasure and comfort.

rationales.security: When Anil suggests they "hold off on any big expenses for a few months," she agrees and accepts a more cautious plan during uncertainty.
rationales.benevolence: She spends days making khichdi, washing sheets, and checking Aarav's temperature while he is sick, centering close family care.

rationales.achievement: The unfinished paneer tikka post, the "traffic dropped forty percent," and her description of the blog as "another thing I am failing at" show a sense of falling short in something tied to competence and output.
rationales.benevolence: Her own writing time is displaced by helping Rhea with her project and tending to family needs first.

rationales.achievement: She "actually finished the paneer tikka blog post" and says it "felt good to hit that button," showing satisfaction in completing and publishing her work.
rationales.security: She renews health insurance, increases coverage to "ten lakhs," and renews Aarav's accident cover to create more protection against future risk.
rationales.tradition: Dinner uses "my grandmother's recipe" with the remembered finishing step, maintaining a family cooking practice.

rationales.achievement: He does "two weekends" of preparation, wins the vote seven to two, and relishes feeling like "the version of me that knows how to move a room."
rationales.power: He says "you control the information, you set the direction," and takes satisfaction in steering the meeting and outcome.

rationales.achievement: He improves the reporting template, the GM notices, and "clean work" matters because it confirms he is someone "who knows how to run operations."
He watches someone else present "my regional consolidation idea" without credit and lets the recognition pass to Daniel.
rationales.power: He "just let it happen" and stays silent while control of the idea and narrative moves to someone else.
rationales.conformity: He suppresses objection because speaking up would have "looked petty," choosing social smoothness over asserting himself.

rationales.power: He is stuck waiting for IT, saying "I have done everything I can do" while a younger coordinator determines when his department can function, highlighting loss of control and influence.
He believes "someone needs to own that decision," but the GM overrides him and he is left anticipating he will clean up the consequences.
rationales.security: He defends the old staffing model because "overtime costs are predictable" and the floor needs "structure," favoring order and stability.
rationales.conformity: Even after objecting, he says "it is what the GM wants, so we execute," falling in line with organizational expectations.

rationales.self_direction: He withholds his real view and says "I just nodded," then frames silence as "you pick your battles" instead of speaking from his own judgment.
rationales.conformity: Throughout the workshop he goes along to avoid "making a scene," prioritizing compliance with the social situation over open disagreement.

rationales.hedonism: The day is built around simple personal pleasures like kaya toast, coffee, reading the paper, F1, and enjoying that "nobody needs anything from you."

rationales.achievement: He watches Daniel receive applause for "my idea" and says work no longer feels like the place "where I mattered," showing loss of accomplishment and recognition.
rationales.power: He says his "decisions moved things" before, but now he only watches others present and feels the title is "a frame around an empty picture," signaling diminished influence.

rationales.tradition: What she wishes she had said centers on the script's "genealogy" and her jiddo's hand guiding hers, treating inherited practice as something meaningful to defend.
She helps preserve jiddo's practice sheets and says giving up calligraphy would feel like "forgetting him," tying her work directly to family continuity and inherited practice.

rationales.stimulation: She resists the proposed experimental direction to "break the script apart" and says even thinking about it makes her stomach tight, showing avoidance of novelty and disruption.
rationales.tradition: Her objection is that Teta would not be able to read it and she keeps picturing jiddo watching, grounding her choice in preserving inherited form and meaning.

rationales.self_direction: She pushes back against others' framing and insists "I just don't think ripping something apart is the only way," asserting her own artistic judgment.
rationales.tradition: Her stance favors understanding and working within the script rather than destroying it, reflecting commitment to an established form.

rationales.self_direction: She says she feels like "no one wonders what I'd do if I chose freely" and decides "So I won't do it," suppressing her own preferred path.
rationales.conformity: She avoids the experimental thesis direction because it would make her grandmother go quiet and her mother "change the subject," choosing family harmony over disruption.
rationales.tradition: The decision is shaped by loyalty to jiddo's story and by refusing to fragment the script in a way her family would experience as hurtful or disloyal.

rationales.self_direction: She experiments alone, keeps the two sheets private, and decides "for now they're mine," holding the choice on her own terms rather than anyone else's.
rationales.stimulation: She makes the letters "drift apart" and loosens the form in a way she has not shown others, actively exploring variation and uncertainty.
rationales.tradition: Even in the experiment, it is still "the bismillah in naskh the way jiddo taught me," and she notes "Teta could still read it" and "Jiddo would have recognized the hand," preserving continuity with inherited practice.
